title: Learning Curves: Tracking Loss Over Time
concept: A learning curve plots training and validation loss over successive epochs, serving as the primary diagnostic tool to detect underfitting and overfitting in deep models.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In which scenario should you NOT shuffle your training data?

options.text: In time series forecasting, where chronological order is a critical feature.
In standard tabular house price prediction.

exercises.explain: Correct! Shuffling time series data breaks the temporal order, ruining the model.

cards.title: The medical monitor
cards.body: A doctor doesn't just guess if a patient is recovering; they check the heart monitor over time. In deep learning, a learning curve is that heart monitor. By plotting the loss score of both training and validation sets over each epoch, we can instantly see if the model is learning, stalling, or overfitting.

cards.title: The Learning Curve
cards.body: A learning curve is a line graph where the horizontal axis is **Epochs** and the vertical axis is **Loss** (error). We plot two lines: **Training Loss** (error on training rows) and **Validation Loss** (error on unseen validation rows).

cards.title: Studying vs. exam performance
cards.body: Think of training loss as your score on practice quizzes (questions you've seen). It should steadily go down (fewer mistakes). Validation loss is your score on the real exams (questions you haven't seen). If your practice score is perfect but your exam score starts getting worse, you are memorizing (overfitting).

cards.title: Reading the curves
cards.body: - **Normal Learning**: Both training and validation loss curves slope downward together, leveling off at a low error rate. - **Overfitting**: Training loss continues to slide toward 0, but the validation loss curve starts bending upward, showing the model is getting worse on new data.

cards.title: Training loss doesn't tell the whole story
cards.body: It's easy to look only at the training loss, see it reach 0.01, and celebrate. In deep learning, a training loss of 0.01 is often a warning sign: it means the model has likely overfit by memorizing the training set. You must *always* cross-reference with the validation curve to confirm true generalization.

left.text:
Both training and validation lines drop.
They stay close and level off.
Model generalizes well.

right.text:
Training line drops to near 0.
Validation line bends upward.
Model has memorized training data.

exercises.prompt: What does an upward bend in the validation loss curve indicate (while training loss continues to drop)?

options.text: The model is overfitting, memorizing training details and losing accuracy on new data.
The model is underfitting and needs to train for more epochs.
The loss function has been successfully optimized to 0.
The batch size is too small.

exercises.explain: Correct! If validation error rises while training error drops, the model is overfitting.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A training loss of 0.001 is a guaranteed sign of a perfect model that will perform well on new test data.
exercises.explain: False! Near-zero training loss often indicates the model has overfit and memorized the training set.

exercises.sentence: A learning curve plots ___ on the vertical axis and epochs on the horizontal axis.
exercises.explain: Learning curves typically plot loss (error) to track optimization progress.

exercises.prompt: Which curve trend represents a healthy, well-generalizing model?

options.text: Both train and validation loss drop together and level off close to each other.
Train loss drops to 0, while validation loss spikes to infinity.
Both curves remain flat at their initial high error value.

exercises.explain: Correct! Curves that decrease together and stabilize represent healthy learning.

exercises.prompt: You are training a deep network. Around epoch 20, the training loss is 0.4 and validation loss is 0.45. By epoch 50, training loss drops to 0.1, but validation loss has climbed to 0.8. At which epoch should you stop training and save your model?

options.text: Around epoch 20, because that was the point before the model began overfitting (where validation loss was at its lowest).
Epoch 50, because training loss is at its lowest.
Epoch 1, to ensure the model has no bias.
Let it run to epoch 100 to see if the validation loss drops again.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The goal is to maximize performance on unseen data. After epoch 20, the validation curve starts climbing, showing the model is overfitting. The best model is captured at the valley of the validation curve. Next, let's learn how we automate this exit using Early Stopping!
